subject: PhD Numerical Analysis
instructions: Do 4 (four) of the first 5 (1-5) and 4 (four) of the last 5 problems (6-10).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Show that \(A\) has a Schur decomposition.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: If \(A\) is normal, show that \(A\) is diagonalizable.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Prove that each principal submatrix of \(A\) is Hermitian positive definite.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Prove that an element of \(A\) with largest magnitude lies on the diagonal.

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Prove that \(A\) has a Cholesky decomposition.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Show that \(\lVert x\rVert_\infty\) is equivalent to \(\lVert x\rVert_2\) for all \(x\in\mathbb{R}^n\). That is to find \(C\) and \(c\) such that \(c\lVert x\rVert_\infty\leq \lVert x\rVert_2\leq C\lVert x\rVert_\infty\), for all \(x\in\mathbb{R}^n\). Note that the constants should be determined so that the equalities hold for some nonzero \(x\in\mathbb{R}^n\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Show that \(\lVert QA\rVert_2=\lVert A\rVert_2\) if \(Q\) is a unitary matrix.

problems.text: Assume that \(A\in\mathbb{C}^{n\times n}\) and there exists \(p\geq 1\) such that \(\lVert A\rVert_p<1\), where \(\lVert\cdot\rVert_p\) is a vector-induced matrix norm.
Let \(A=U\Sigma V^*\) be the singular value decomposition of \(A\in\mathbb{C}^{m\times n}\). Let \(u_j\) denote column \(j\) of \(U\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Suppose \(\operatorname{rank}(A)=p<n<m\). Show \(\{u_1,u_2,\ldots,u_p\}\) is a basis for \(\operatorname{Col}(A)\) and \(\{u_{p+1},u_{p+2},\ldots,u_m\}\) is a basis for \(\operatorname{Null}(A^*)\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text:
Suppose \(A\) is full rank and \(x\neq 0\). Let \(\sigma_i\), \(i=1,\ldots,n\), be the singular values of \(A\). Show
\[
\sigma_1\geq \frac{\lVert Ax\rVert_2}{\lVert x\rVert_2}\geq \sigma_n>0.
\]
If you want to use the property that \(\lVert A\rVert_2=\sigma_1\), then you must prove that it holds.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Prove that \(g\) is a contraction on \(G=[\ln 1.1,\ln 3]\).

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Prove that \(x_{k+1}=g(x_k)\) converges to an unique fixed point \(z\in G=[\ln 1.1,\ln 3]\) for any initial value \(x_0\in G=[\ln 1.1,\ln 3]\).

problems.text: Based on \(u_1(x)=1\), \(u_2(x)=x\), \(u_3(x)=x^2\), use Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization process to compute the three polynomials \(w_1(x)\), \(w_2(x)\), \(w_3(x)\) which are orthonormal on the interval \([0,1]\) with respect to the inner product \((f,g)=\int_0^1 f(x)g(x)\,dx\). Using these polynomials, find the best approximation in \(P^2[0,1]\) for \(f(x)=x^{1/2}\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Derive this formula by using Taylor’s theorem.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Derive this formula by using Lagrange polynomial representation.

problems.text:
Assume the numerical quadrature for \(\hat f(\hat x)\) on \([0,1]\) is
\[
\hat J(\hat f)=\int_0^1\hat f(\hat x)\,d\hat x\approx \hat Q(\hat f)=\sum_{j=0}^m\hat\alpha_j\hat f(\hat x_j).
\]
Derive the numerical quadrature of \(J(f)=\int_a^b f(x)\,dx\).

Consider numerically solving the initial value problem
\[
y'(t)=f(t,y),\qquad 0<t\leq t_f,\qquad \text{with }y(0)=\eta.
\]
Assume \(f\) is sufficiently differentiable and let \(h\) denote the step size. Show that all convergent members of the family of methods
\[
y_{n+2}+(\theta-2)y_{n+1}+(1-\theta)y_n=\frac14h\bigl[(6+\theta)f_{n+2}+3(\theta-2)f_n\bigr],
\]
parameterized by \(\theta\), are also \(A_0\)-stable.
